content:
# Leviticus Chapter 6 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 60r–60v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_006_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_006_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 90–91 (pa_0090–pa_0091)

---

**8** The LORD spoke to Moses, saying,

**9** "Command Aaron and his sons, saying: This is the instruction of the burnt offering. The burnt offering shall be on the hearth on the altar all night until the morning, and the fire of the altar shall be kept burning on it.

**10** And the priest shall put on his linen garment and put his linen undergarment on his body, and he shall take up the ashes to which the fire has reduced the burnt offering on the altar and put them beside the altar.

**11** Then he shall take off his garments and put on other garments and carry the ashes outside the camp to a clean place.

**12** The fire on the altar shall be kept burning on it; it shall not go out. The priest shall burn wood on it every morning, and he shall arrange the burnt offering on it and shall burn on it the fat of the peace offerings.

**13** Fire shall be kept burning on the altar continually; it shall not go out.

**14** "And this is the instruction of the grain offering. The sons of Aaron shall offer it before the LORD, in front of the altar.

**15** And one of them shall take up from it a handful of the fine flour of the grain offering and of its oil and all the frankincense that is on the grain offering and shall burn this as its memorial portion on the altar, a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

**16** And the remainder of it Aaron and his sons shall eat. It shall be eaten without leaven in a holy place. In the court of the tent of meeting they shall eat it.

**17** It shall not be baked with leaven. I have given it as their portion of my fire offerings. It is most holy, like the sin offering and like the guilt offering.

**18** Every male among the sons of Aaron may eat of it. It is a perpetual statute throughout your generations, from the LORD's fire offerings. Everyone who touches them shall become holy."

**19** The LORD spoke to Moses, saying,

**20** "This is the offering that Aaron and his sons shall offer to the LORD on the day when he is anointed: a tenth of an ephah of fine flour as a regular grain offering, half of it in the morning and half of it in the evening.

**21** It shall be made with oil on a griddle. You shall bring it well mixed, in baked pieces like a grain offering, and you shall offer it as a pleasing aroma to the LORD.

**22** The priest who is anointed in his place from among his sons shall offer it. It is a perpetual statute to the LORD; it shall be burned in its entirety.

**23** Every grain offering of a priest shall be burned in its entirety; it shall not be eaten."

**24** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,

**25** "Speak to Aaron and his sons, saying: This is the instruction of the sin offering. In the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered, the sin offering shall be slaughtered before the LORD; it is most holy.

**26** The priest who offers it for sin shall eat it. In a holy place it shall be eaten, in the court of the tent of meeting.

**27** Whatever touches its flesh shall become holy. And when any of its blood is sprinkled on a garment, you shall wash that on which it was sprinkled in a holy place.

**28** And the earthen vessel in which it is boiled shall be broken. But if it is boiled in a bronze vessel, that shall be scoured and rinsed in water.

**29** Every male among the priests may eat of it; it is most holy.

**30** But no sin offering shall be eaten from which any blood is brought into the tent of meeting to make atonement in the Holy Place; it shall be burned with fire.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 60r–60v.** Both manuscript pages were examined directly. Folio 60r opens with the close of the chapter 5 material and transitions immediately into the beginning of chapter 6 at what the MT numbers 6:1 (English 6:8). The visual break between chapter 5 and chapter 6 in the manuscript is subtle — there is no major parasha break (setumah or petuchah) at the English chapter boundary between what the English Bible calls 5:19 and 6:8, since the MT chapter numbering differs from the English. The MT begins chapter 6 at English 5:20 (the false-oath section of the guilt offering), but the task follows English versification beginning at 6:8 (MT 6:1): the priestly instruction sections addressed specifically to Aaron and his sons — marked by the distinctive formula צַו אֶת-אַהֲרֹן וְאֶת-בָּנָיו (tsav et-Aharon ve-et-banav, "Command Aaron and his sons"), rather than the more common "Speak to the sons of Israel."

The shift to tsav ("command") rather than dabber / emor ("speak / say") is deliberate and significant: these instructions are addressed to the priests as cultic functionaries, not to the laity as covenant community members. The manuscript makes visible the four-unit structure of the chapter: (1) the burnt offering torah (vv. 8–13/1–6), characterized by the esh tamid legislation; (2) the grain offering torah (vv. 14–18/7–11), governing the priestly portion; (3) the high priest's daily grain offering (vv. 19–23/12–16); and (4) the sin offering torah (vv. 24–30/17–23), governing most-holy-thing consumption and blood-contamination procedures.

The repeated formula זֹאת תּוֹרַת (zot torat, "this is the instruction of") in vv. 9, 14, and 25 is a prominent structural marker in the manuscript, introducing each new section of priestly procedure. The phrase esh tamid (vv. 12–13) stands out as a single powerful clause — the fire on the altar shall not go out — which is stated twice within two verses with slight variation, providing a kind of emphatic inclusio around the wood-stacking and fat-burning instructions of v. 12.

Folio 60v continues the chapter from approximately v. 14 or 15 onward through the end of v. 30. The manuscript clearly shows the high-priest's offering section (vv. 19–23), which is introduced by a second vayyedabber YHWH el-Moshe formula (v. 19), distinguishing it from the preceding grain offering section. The phrase qodesh qodashim (most holy / holy of holies, used substantively to describe the sacrificial category) is visible in the manuscript at vv. 17, 25, and 29 as a recurring marker of sacral status.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 90–91 (LXX, pa_0090–pa_0091).** Both Vaticanus pages were examined directly. Page 90 (pa_0090) carries the upper portion of the Leviticus 6 LXX text, including the beginning of the burnt offering instruction section, in the characteristic fourth-century uncial three-column hand. The chapter heading in the outer margin appears to read CAP. XXXIII (or similar), consistent with the LXX enumeration for this portion of Leviticus. Page 91 (pa_0091) continues the LXX text through the remainder of the chapter. Both pages are in good condition with readable columns throughout.

The LXX text of Leviticus 6 (LXX 6:1–23, corresponding to English 6:8–30) follows the MT structure closely with no major textual dislocations. The most significant structural divergence is that the LXX chapter division for Leviticus 6 begins at the equivalent of English 6:1 (the false-oath section), matching the MT chapter beginning at 5:20, rather than the English chapter boundary. Within the shared material (English 6:8–30), the LXX witnesses the same four-unit structure and the same key formulae: the τοῦτος ὁ νόμος formula (for zot torat), πῦρ διαπαντός (for esh tamid), and ἅγια τῶν ἁγίων (for qodesh qodashim). These are examined in detail in the variants table below.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **Zot torat ha-olah / "This is the instruction of the burnt offering" (v. 9).** Hebrew זֹאת תּוֹרַת הָעֹלָה (zot torat ha-olah). The word torah (תּוֹרָה) here is used in its basic pre-canonical sense: instruction, procedure, ruling, or regulation — not the Torah as a whole. It is the same root as Leviticus's repeated use of torat ha-chattat (instruction of the sin offering), torat ha-asham (instruction of the guilt offering), and so on throughout chapters 6–7. This use of torah as "procedural instruction" for a specific cultic act is found throughout the priestly literature and is a reminder that the primary sense of the root ירה (yarah, "to teach, to direct") is directional guidance — here the specific sequence of actions required of the priest for each type of offering.

   "Instruction" (ESV: "law"; NASB: "law"; NIV: "regulations"; NJPS: "ritual") is the rendering chosen here. ESV and NASB use "law," which is a fair rendering but carries inevitable canonical overtones. "Instruction" more precisely captures the procedural, directive character of torat here and distinguishes it from "the Law" as a canonical whole. The LXX renders οὗτος ὁ νόμος ("this is the law"), using νόμος — the standard Greek word for law/regulation — which is the LXX's consistent rendering of torah. This translation renders torat as "instruction" throughout chapter 6 to preserve the procedural character of the Hebrew, while noting the LXX's νόμος rendering.

2. **Esh tamid / "Fire shall be kept burning continually" (vv. 12–13).** Hebrew אֵשׁ תָּמִיד תּוּקַד עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ לֹא תִכְבֶּה (esh tamid tuqad al-ha-mizbeach lo tikhbeh — "a fire continual shall be kept burning on the altar; it shall not go out"). The phrase esh tamid ("perpetual/continual fire") is one of the most theologically iconic phrases in the entire Levitical cult manual. The adjective tamid ("continual, perpetual, regular, always") governs the entire altar-fire theology: the fire that descended from the LORD's presence (Lev 9:24) must never be extinguished, because the altar fire is the medium through which all burnt offerings ascend as pleasing aroma. The priest is responsible for maintaining the fire by adding wood every morning (v. 12) and arranging the burnt offering upon it.

   The phrase is stated twice in vv. 12 and 13 with slight syntactic variation — v. 12 within the positive description of the morning woodstacking, v. 13 as an independent summary statement — providing a structural emphasis. "Fire shall be kept burning on the altar continually; it shall not go out" (v. 13) renders the MT closely. The prohibition lo tikhbeh ("it shall not go out") uses the same root כבה (kavah, "to be extinguished") used throughout biblical poetry and prophecy for the extinction of light, life, and hope — the strongest word for "going out." "Shall not go out" (ESV, NASB, KJV) preserves this force. The LXX renders πῦρ διαπαντὸς καυθήσεται ἐπὶ τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου, οὐ σβεσθήσεται ("a fire always/throughout shall be burning on the altar; it shall not be extinguished"), using σβεννύω ("to quench, extinguish") for kavah — close agreement.

3. **"On the hearth on the altar" (v. 9).** Hebrew עַל-מוֹקְדָה עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (al-moqdah al-ha-mizbeach). The word מוֹקֵד (moqed, from the root יקד, to burn/kindle) refers to the burning-place or hearth of the altar — the specific location on the altar surface where the fire burns and where the burnt offering lies overnight. "Hearth" (ESV, NASB) renders moqed accurately. The LXX renders ἐπὶ τῆς καύσεως ("upon the burning / upon the burning-place"), using the same root as for the fire more broadly, which is a slightly less specific rendering but captures the idea of the fiery place.

4. **"The ashes to which the fire has reduced the burnt offering" (v. 10).** Hebrew וְרֵם אֶת-הַדֶּשֶׁן אֲשֶׁר תֹּאכַל הָאֵשׁ אֶת-הָעֹלָה עַל-הַמִּזְבֵּחַ (ve-rem et-ha-deshen asher to'khal ha-esh et-ha-olah al-ha-mizbeach — "and he shall lift up / remove the ashes that the fire has consumed the burnt offering on the altar"). The Hebrew uses the graphic anthropomorphic verb to'khal ("has devoured/consumed") for the fire's action on the offering. Deshen (דֶּשֶׁן) denotes the fatty ashes left after the burning of the offering — not simply any ash, but specifically the residue of the fat and the sacrificial body. The priest takes up these specific ashes and places them beside the altar before the more complete removal outside the camp.

   "The ashes to which the fire has reduced the burnt offering" (ESV: "the ashes that the fire has consumed of the burnt offering"; NASB similarly) renders the participial construction. The graphic "consumed" or "devoured" quality of to'khal is slightly softened in translation but the process is clear. The LXX renders τὴν κατακαυθεῖσαν κατακαύματα ὁλοκαυτώματος ("the burnt ashes of the whole burnt offering"), using κατακαύματα (ashes from burning / burnt remnants), in close agreement.

5. **"Linen garment" / "linen undergarment" (v. 10).** Hebrew מִדּוֹ בַד (middo vad — "his garment of linen") and מִכְנְסֵי-בַד (mikhnesei-vad — "linen undergarments"). The priestly linen vestments are specified precisely: the outer garment (middah) and the undergarments (mikhnesaim) covering from waist to thigh, worn specifically for the ash-removal procedure. These are the same garments described in Exodus 28:39–42. The specification that the priest wear linen during the ash-removal service emphasizes the holy character of even this seemingly menial task: taking up the ashes is a priestly act, not a custodial one. "Linen garment" and "linen undergarment" (ESV, NASB) render the Hebrew precisely. The LXX renders χιτῶνα ποδήρη λινοῦν ("a full-length linen tunic") and τὰ περισκελῆ τὰ λινᾶ ("the linen leg coverings"), in close agreement.

6. **"He shall arrange the burnt offering on it" (v. 12).** Hebrew וְעָרַךְ עָלֶיהָ הָעֹלָה (ve-arakh aleha ha-olah — "and he shall arrange / set in order the burnt offering upon it"). The verb arak (עָרַךְ, "to arrange, to set in order") is the technical term used in Leviticus 1 for the arrangement of the pieces of the burnt offering on the altar — every part has its specific place. In v. 12 it describes the daily morning arrangement of the burnt offering upon the freshly stocked altar fire after the ashes have been removed and new wood added. "Shall arrange" (ESV, NASB: "shall lay") renders the verb precisely. The LXX renders ἐπιθήσει ("shall place/put upon"), using the general "place" verb. "Arrange" is more precise to the MT's technical vocabulary.

7. **"Fat of the peace offerings" / "fat of the shelamim" (v. 12).** Hebrew חֵלֶב הַשְּׁלָמִים (chelev ha-shelamim — "the fat of the peace offerings"). The burning of the fat portions of the shelamim (peace/fellowship offerings) on the altar fire is described as a concurrent morning activity — alongside the burnt offering arrangement. This reflects the regular daily schedule: the priest stokes the fire, arranges the daily burnt offering, and also burns the fat portions from previous-day peace offerings that have been set aside. "Peace offerings" continues the established rendering of shelamim (from shalom). The LXX renders τὰ στέατα τοῦ σωτηρίου ("the fat portions of the salvation/peace offering"), using σωτήριον ("salvation/peace offering") for shelamim — the standard LXX rendering.

8. **"It shall be eaten without leaven in a holy place" (v. 16).** Hebrew בְּמָקוֹם קָדוֹשׁ תֵּאָכֵל מַצּוֹת תֵּאָכֵל (be-maqom qadosh te'akhel matsot te'akhel — "in a holy place it shall be eaten; without leaven it shall be eaten"). The unusual double statement of te'akhel ("it shall be eaten") — once with the locative specification (in a holy place) and once with the manner specification (without leaven) — is a clear emphatic construction in the MT. The two prohibitions are distinct: spatial restriction (the priestly portion may only be consumed in the court of the tent of meeting, v. 16b) and material restriction (it must be unleavened). "It shall be eaten without leaven in a holy place" conflates the two clauses into one for smooth English, following the standard English versions. The double emphasis is noted here.

9. **"It is most holy, like the sin offering and like the guilt offering" (v. 17).** Hebrew קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים הִוא כַּחַטָּאת וְכָאָשָׁם (qodesh qodashim hi ka-chattat ve-kha-asham — "most holy it is, like the sin offering and like the guilt offering"). The Hebrew superlative qodesh qodashim (literally "holy of holies" — the same construction as the inner sanctuary itself, the Most Holy Place) is the highest sacral category in the Levitical system. Applied to the grain offering priest's portion, this declaration signals that even the remainder of the grain offering — though ordinary grain and oil — is elevated to the most-holy category by virtue of being a portion of a fire offering presented to the LORD.

   "Most holy" (ESV, NASB, KJV) renders qodesh qodashim consistently. The comparison "like the sin offering and like the guilt offering" (ka-chattat ve-kha-asham) places the grain offering priest's portion on the same sacral level as the chattat and asham portions, which are the paradigmatic most-holy offerings. The LXX renders ἅγια τῶν ἁγίων ἐστίν ("it is holy of holies / most holy") in direct agreement with the MT superlative structure.

10. **"Everyone who touches them shall become holy" (v. 18).** Hebrew כָּל-אֲשֶׁר-יִגַּע בָּהֶם יִקְדָּשׁ (kol-asher-yigga bam yiqdash — "everyone/everything that touches them shall become holy"). This is one of the most debated phrases in Leviticus: does it mean that any unauthorized person who touches the most-holy things becomes holy (i.e., is consecrated — which would be dangerous or impermissible), or that the most-holy things consecrate / make holy whatever they contact (i.e., anything in contact with them is rendered holy and therefore reserved for God)?

   The likely meaning is the latter: the most-holy offerings are so charged with sacral status that contact with them renders the thing touched holy — meaning it now belongs to the sacred sphere and cannot revert to common use. This is a kind of contagious holiness: objects that contact the altar, the offerings, the priestly vestments during service are rendered holy and may not be treated as ordinary. The principle extends to garments splattered with sin offering blood (v. 27) and the vessels used to boil the sin offering (v. 28). "Shall become holy" renders yiqdash (qal imperfect of qadash) accurately. The LXX renders ἁγιασθήσεται ("shall be made holy / shall be sanctified"), the future passive, in close agreement.

11. **"This is the offering that Aaron and his sons shall offer to the LORD on the day when he is anointed" (v. 20).** Hebrew זֶה קָרְבַּן אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו אֲשֶׁר-יַקְרִיבוּ לַיהוָה בְּיוֹם הִמָּשַׁח אֹתוֹ (zeh qorban Aharon u-vanav asher-yaqribu la-YHWH be-yom himashach oto — "this is the offering of Aaron and his sons that they shall bring to the LORD on the day of his being anointed"). The phrase be-yom himashach oto ("on the day of his being anointed") is grammatically unusual and has been interpreted in two ways: (a) the regular daily offering of the high priest from the day of his anointing onward, or (b) an inaugural offering on the actual day of anointing. The broader context, including v. 22's reference to "the priest who is anointed in his place from among his sons," supports interpretation (a): the high priest's daily grain offering as an ongoing institution from the day of anointing — a tamid ("regular/perpetual") offering for the high priest, parallel to the tamid burnt offering and the tamid fire.

   "On the day when he is anointed" (ESV, NASB) follows the more literal rendering. The LXX renders ἐν ᾗ ἂν ἡμέρᾳ χρίσῃς αὐτόν ("on whatever day you anoint him"), using the active subjunctive — the same event but from the perspective of the one who performs the anointing rather than the one anointed. This shift in perspective makes the Mosaic instruction slightly more conditional (on whatever day the anointing occurs). The MT's passive himashach ("being anointed") is more institutionally neutral.

12. **"Baked pieces like a grain offering" / "well mixed" (v. 21).** Hebrew מֻרְבֶּכֶת תְּבִיאֶנָּה תֻּפִינֵי מִנְחַת פִּתִּים תַּקְרִיב (murbbekhet tevi'enah tuffinei minchat pittim taqriv — "mixed/well-kneaded you shall bring it, in baked crumbled-pieces of the grain offering you shall offer it"). The word מֻרְבֶּכֶת (murbbekhet) is a hapax legomenon (appearing only here in the Hebrew Bible), derived from a root meaning to stir, mix, or combine — typically applied to a rich, well-blended dough or preparation. The phrase tuffinei minchat pittim is also unusual: pittim may denote small pieces, crumbles, or broken portions of a baked product. Together, the verse describes the high priest's daily grain offering as a carefully prepared, well-mixed, fragmented baked offering — not a solid loaf but a crumbled or broken cake, presented in pieces.

   "Well mixed, in baked pieces" (ESV: "well mixed in pieces"; NASB: "well stirred, you shall bring it in baked pieces") renders the passage adequately, acknowledging the uncertain character of murbbekhet. The LXX renders πεφυραμένη ("kneaded/mixed") for murbbekhet and κλάσματα ("broken pieces / fragments") for pittim, confirming the crumbled-offering interpretation. This translation follows the standard rendering with a note on the hapax.

13. **"Every grain offering of a priest shall be burned in its entirety; it shall not be eaten" (v. 23).** Hebrew וְכָל-מִנְחַת כֹּהֵן כָּלִיל תִּהְיֶה לֹא תֵאָכֵל (ve-khol-minchat kohen kalil tihyeh lo te'akhel — "and every grain offering of a priest shall be whole/entire; it shall not be eaten"). The word כָּלִיל (kalil, "whole, entire, complete") is used here for an offering that is wholly consumed — nothing is retained, nothing given to the priests. This is the key distinction of the priestly grain offering from the lay grain offering: whereas the layperson's grain offering gives a memorial portion to the altar and the remainder to the priests (vv. 15–16), the priest's own grain offering belongs wholly to the LORD in its entirety (kalil), because the priest cannot simultaneously be the offerer and the beneficiary of his own offering's remainder. "Shall be burned in its entirety; it shall not be eaten" renders kalil and lo te'akhel accurately. The LXX renders ὁλοκαύτωμα ἔσται ("it shall be a whole burnt offering / a holocaust"), using ὁλοκαύτωμα ("wholly burned offering"), in close agreement.

14. **"This is the instruction of the sin offering" (v. 25).** Hebrew זֹאת תּוֹרַת הַחַטָּאת (zot torat ha-chattat — "this is the instruction of the sin offering"). The third use of the formula zot torat introduces the most-holy-things legislation for the sin offering, specifically governing how the sin offering flesh is to be consumed by the officiating priest. This section (vv. 24–30) contains some of the most technically specific purity legislation in Leviticus, dealing with contagious holiness (v. 27), vessel defilement (v. 28), and the special case of inner-sanctuary blood sin offerings that cannot be eaten at all (v. 30). "This is the instruction" follows the rendering established for v. 9.

15. **"Most holy" / qodesh qodashim throughout (vv. 17, 25, 29).** The phrase qodesh qodashim ("holy of holies / most holy") appears three times in this chapter, designating the grain offering priestly portion (v. 17), the sin offering (v. 25), and the sin offering portion eligible for priestly consumption (v. 29). In each case the superlative construction signals the highest level of sacral status in the Levitical hierarchy, requiring exclusive priestly consumption in the court of the tent of meeting (v. 16, 26). "Most holy" is the standard rendering (ESV, NASB, KJV) and is used consistently throughout. The LXX renders ἅγια τῶν ἁγίων throughout, in direct agreement.

16. **"In the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered, the sin offering shall be slaughtered" (v. 25).** Hebrew בִּמְקוֹם אֲשֶׁר תִּשָּׁחֵט הָעֹלָה תִּשָּׁחֵט הַחַטָּאת לִפְנֵי יְהוָה (bi-maqom asher tisshachet ha-olah tisshachet ha-chattat lifne YHWH — "in the place that the burnt offering is slaughtered, the sin offering shall be slaughtered before the LORD"). The slaughtering location is specified to align the sin offering precisely with the burnt offering's slaughter site — both are to be killed in the same area of the court, before the LORD, at the north side of the altar (see Lev 1:11). The spatial specification emphasizes the sacred character of the sin offering: it is not a lesser sacrifice to be dispatched casually but a most-holy offering whose slaughter must occur in the same consecrated space as the premier sacrifice.

   "In the place where the burnt offering is slaughtered" renders the construction literally. The LXX renders ἐν τόπῳ οὗ σφάζουσιν τὸ ὁλοκαύτωμα σφάξουσιν τὰ περὶ ἁμαρτίας ("in the place where they slaughter the whole burnt offering, they shall slaughter the things concerning sin"), in close agreement.

17. **"When any of its blood is sprinkled on a garment" (v. 27).** Hebrew וַאֲשֶׁר יִזֶּה מִדָּמָהּ עַל-הַבֶּגֶד (va-asher yizzeh mi-damah al-ha-beged — "and whatever is sprinkled from its blood upon the garment"). The verb yizzeh (from nazah/nizzah, "to spatter, to sprinkle") describes blood spattering accidentally onto garments during the sin offering ritual — presumably during slaughter or blood manipulation. The instruction that such a garment must be washed in a holy place (v. 27b) reflects the principle that the sin offering's blood is so charged with most-holy status that even accidental contact creates an obligation. "When any of its blood is sprinkled on a garment" renders the conditional clause accurately. The LXX renders ὅς ἐὰν ῥαντισθῇ ἀπὸ τοῦ αἵματος ἐπὶ τὸ ἱμάτιον ("whoever/whatever is sprinkled from its blood upon the garment"), in close agreement.

18. **"An earthen vessel shall be broken" / "a bronze vessel shall be scoured and rinsed" (v. 28).** Hebrew וּכְלִי-חֶרֶשׂ אֲשֶׁר תְּבֻשַּׁל-בּוֹ יִשָּׁבֵר וְאִם-בִּכְלִי נְחֹשֶׁת בֻּשָּׁלָה וּמֹרַק וְשֻׁטַּף בַּמָּיִם (u-kheli-cheres asher tebushal-bo yishshaver ve-im-bi-kheli nechoshet bushshah u-moraq ve-shuktaf ba-mayim — "and an earthen vessel in which it has been boiled shall be broken, and if in a bronze vessel it was boiled, it shall be scoured and rinsed in water"). The distinction between earthen vessels (which are porous and absorb the most-holy sin offering's essence permanently — hence must be broken) and bronze vessels (which can be scoured and rinsed and thus purified — hence may be reused) reflects the Levitical understanding of how holiness and impurity are transmitted through contact and absorption.

   The verb moraq (מֹרַק, "to scour, to scrub clean") is a hapax in Leviticus and describes the intensive scouring required for a metal vessel. "Scoured and rinsed in water" renders moraq ve-shukhtaf ba-mayim accurately. The LXX renders ἐκχεθήσεται καὶ ἀποπλυθήσεται ὕδατι ("shall be poured out and washed with water"), which simplifies the process and loses the specific scouring nuance of moraq in favor of a simpler washing. This translation follows the MT's more specific vocabulary.

19. **"But no sin offering shall be eaten from which any blood is brought into the tent of meeting to make atonement in the Holy Place; it shall be burned with fire" (v. 30).** Hebrew וְכָל-חַטָּאת אֲשֶׁר יוּבָא מִדָּמָהּ אֶל-אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד לְכַפֵּר בַּקֹּדֶשׁ לֹא תֵאָכֵל בָּאֵשׁ תִּשָּׂרֵף (ve-khol-chattat asher yuva mi-damah el-ohel mo'ed le-khapper ba-qodesh lo te'akhel ba-esh tissaref — "and every sin offering from which its blood is brought into the tent of meeting to make atonement in the Holy Place shall not be eaten; with fire it shall be burned"). This final verse of the chapter creates a principled exception to the rule that the officiating priest eats the sin offering: when a sin offering's blood is brought inside the tent of meeting for the purpose of making atonement in the Holy Place — i.e., the Day of Atonement type of inner-sanctuary blood offering — the flesh of such an offering is wholly burned, not eaten. This mirrors the rule for the anointed priest's own sin offering (Lev 4:3–12) and the congregation's sin offering (Lev 4:13–21), where the blood is brought inside the tent.

   "To make atonement in the Holy Place" (ba-qodesh le-khapper) renders the phrase accurately: ba-qodesh is the Holy Place (the inner sanctuary) where atonement blood is applied. "It shall be burned with fire" renders ba-esh tissaref — the emphatic specification of complete incineration, matching the disposal instructions for the inner-sanctuary sin offerings of chapter 4. The LXX renders ἐν πυρὶ κατακαήσεται ("shall be burned down with fire"), in close agreement.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants (pp. 90–91, pa_0090–pa_0091) — Significant Divergences from MT

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 6:9 | zot torat ha-olah ("this is the instruction of the burnt offering") | οὗτος ὁ νόμος τῆς ὁλοκαυτώσεως ("this is the law of the whole burnt offering") | LXX renders torah with νόμος ("law/ordinance"), its standard rendering throughout. For ha-olah, LXX uses ὁλοκαύτωσις ("whole burning"), the standard LXX term for the olah. Close agreement on structure. |
| 6:9 | al-moqdah ("on the hearth") | ἐπὶ τῆς καύσεως ("upon the burning-place") | LXX uses καύσεως (from καίω, "to burn") for moqed (from יקד, "to burn") — same root concept, different lexeme. Close semantic agreement. |
| 6:10 | ve-rem et-ha-deshen ("and he shall take up the ashes / remove the fat-ashes") | καὶ ἀνελεῖ τὴν κατακαυθεῖσαν κατάκαυσιν ("and he shall take away the burned ashes") | LXX uses ἀναιρέω ("to take up, remove") for rum (hiphil of rum, "to lift, remove"), in close agreement. The deshen (fatty ashes) is rendered κατακαυθεῖσαν κατάκαυσιν ("the burned burning"), using a cognate construction parallel to the Hebrew deshen. |
| 6:11 | ve-hotsi et-ha-deshen ("and he shall carry out the ashes") | ἐξοίσουσιν τὴν κατακαυθεῖσαν κατάκαυσιν ("they shall carry out the burned ashes") | LXX shifts from singular (the priest, MT) to plural ("they shall carry out"), a minor divergence. MT reads singular throughout the priestly instruction sections; LXX's plural may generalize the priestly action. |
| 6:12 | ve-arakh aleha ha-olah ("and he shall arrange the burnt offering upon it") | καὶ ἐπιθήσει ἐπ᾽ αὐτὸ ὁλοκαύτωμα ("and he shall place upon it the whole burnt offering") | LXX uses ἐπιθήσει ("shall place upon") for arakh ("shall arrange"), losing the specific technical nuance of arrangement/ordering. MT's arakh specifies the careful laying-out of the offering pieces in order. |
| 6:12 | esh tamid tuqad al-ha-mizbeach ("a continual fire shall be kept burning on the altar") | πῦρ διαπαντὸς καυθήσεται ἐπὶ τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου ("a fire always shall be burning on the altar") | LXX renders tamid with διαπαντός ("throughout / always"), its standard rendering of tamid. Full agreement on the perpetual-fire concept. |
| 6:13 | lo tikhbeh ("it shall not go out") | οὐ σβεσθήσεται ("it shall not be extinguished") | LXX uses σβεννύω ("to quench, extinguish") for kavah ("to go out, be extinguished"), a close semantic equivalent. |
| 6:15 | kol-ha-levonah ("all the frankincense") | πᾶν τὸ λίβανον ("all the frankincense") | Direct agreement. The entire frankincense portion placed on the grain offering is to be burned as the memorial portion. |
| 6:16 | be-maqom qadosh ("in a holy place") | ἐν τόπῳ ἁγίῳ ("in a holy place") | Direct agreement. |
| 6:16 | matsot te'akhel ("it shall be eaten without leaven") | ἄζυμα βρωθήσεται ("it shall be eaten unleavened") | LXX renders matsot with ἄζυμα ("unleavened"), its standard rendering throughout Leviticus (and Exodus/Numbers). Full agreement. |
| 6:17 | qodesh qodashim hi ("it is most holy") | ἅγια τῶν ἁγίων ἐστίν ("it is holy of holies / most holy") | LXX renders the Hebrew superlative literally as a genitive of specification, ἅγια τῶν ἁγίων — exactly matching the Hebrew construct chain qodesh qodashim. Full structural agreement. |
| 6:18 | kol-zakhar bi-vene Aharon ("every male among the sons of Aaron") | πᾶν ἀρσενικὸν ἐκ τῶν υἱῶν Ααρων ("every male of the sons of Aaron") | LXX renders zakhar with ἀρσενικόν ("male"), the standard LXX rendering. Full agreement. |
| 6:18 | yiqdash ("shall become holy") | ἁγιασθήσεται ("shall be made holy / sanctified") | LXX uses the passive of ἁγιάζω ("to sanctify, to make holy") for qadash, accurately rendering the idea of becoming holy through contact. Full agreement. |
| 6:20 | bi-yom himashach oto ("on the day of his being anointed") | ἐν ᾗ ἂν ἡμέρᾳ χρίσῃς αὐτόν ("on whatever day you anoint him") | LXX shifts from the passive construction (himashach, "his being anointed") to the active second-person subjunctive (χρίσῃς, "you anoint him"), addressing Moses directly. The MT is more impersonal; the LXX makes the Mosaic address explicit. Minor divergence. |
| 6:21 | murbbekhet ("well-mixed / kneaded") | πεφυραμένη ("kneaded") | LXX renders the hapax murbbekhet with πεφυραμένη (perfect passive participle of φύρω, "to mix, knead"), in close agreement with the mixing/kneading sense of the Hebrew root. |
| 6:21 | pittim ("pieces / crumbled portions") | κλάσματα ("broken pieces / fragments") | LXX renders pittim with κλάσματα (from κλάω, "to break"), suggesting the broken/crumbled-piece interpretation of pittim. Close agreement. |
| 6:22 | le-olam ("a perpetual statute / forever") | νόμιμον αἰώνιον ("an eternal ordinance") | LXX renders le-olam with αἰώνιον ("eternal, everlasting"), its standard rendering of olam in a temporal-legislative context. Full agreement. |
| 6:23 | kalil tihyeh lo te'akhel ("it shall be whole; it shall not be eaten") | ὁλόκαυτον ἔσται, οὐ βρωθήσεται ("it shall be a holocaust/whole offering; it shall not be eaten") | LXX renders kalil ("entire/whole") with ὁλόκαυτον ("wholly burned"), aligning the priest's grain offering with the whole burnt offering category. MT's kalil does not necessarily mean "burned" but "complete/entire" — the LXX interpretation implies complete incineration, which is the point, but the term ὁλόκαυτον imports an olah/burnt-offering category marker. |
| 6:25 | zot torat ha-chattat ("this is the instruction of the sin offering") | οὗτος ὁ νόμος τῆς περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας ("this is the law of the [offering] concerning sin") | LXX renders chattat with περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας ("concerning sin"), its consistent periphrastic rendering of chattat throughout Leviticus. Full structural agreement otherwise. |
| 6:27 | yiqdash ("shall become holy" — contagious holiness) | ἁγιασθήσεται ("shall be made holy") | LXX renders consistently with v. 18. The contagious holiness principle is preserved. |
| 6:28 | u-moraq ve-shukhtaf ("and it shall be scoured and rinsed") | ἐκχεθήσεται καὶ ἀποπλυθήσεται ("it shall be poured out and washed") | LXX simplifies: ἐκχεθήσεται ("shall be poured out") for moraq ("scoured") loses the scouring specificity; ἀποπλυθήσεται ("shall be washed away") for shuktaf ("rinsed") is close. LXX reduces the two-stage process. The MT's more detailed procedure is followed in this translation. |
| 6:30 | ba-qodesh ("in the Holy Place") | ἐν τῷ ἁγίῳ ("in the Holy [Place]") | LXX renders ba-qodesh with ἐν τῷ ἁγίῳ, a direct article-plus-adjective construction equivalent to the MT's ba-qodesh. Full agreement. |
| 6:30 | ba-esh tissaref ("with fire it shall be burned") | ἐν πυρὶ κατακαήσεται ("with fire it shall be burned down / wholly burned") | LXX uses κατακαίω ("to burn down, to burn wholly") rather than simply "burned," adding emphasis on complete incineration. Close agreement with the MT's intent. |

### Consistent Term Renderings (Leviticus Series — Updated with Chapter 6)

| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| תּוֹרָה (torah, cultic) | instruction | Procedural directive for a specific offering type; distinct from "the Torah" as canonical whole; LXX: νόμος |
| אֵשׁ תָּמִיד (esh tamid) | fire ... continually / perpetual fire | The unquenchable altar fire; not to go out; LXX: πῦρ διαπαντός |
| מוֹקְדָה (moqedah) | hearth | The burning-place on the altar; LXX: καύσεως |
| דֶּשֶׁן (deshen) | ashes (fat-ashes) | Fatty residue of the burnt offering; LXX: κατακαυθεῖσαν κατάκαυσιν |
| כָּלִיל (kalil) | in its entirety / wholly | Complete consumption; priestly grain offering offered entire; LXX: ὁλόκαυτον |
| קֹדֶשׁ קָדָשִׁים (qodesh qodashim) | most holy | Supreme sacral category; consumed in holy place by priests only; LXX: ἅγια τῶν ἁγίων |
| מַצּוֹת (matsot) | without leaven | Leavening excluded from most-holy grain portions; LXX: ἄζυμα |
| מֹרַק (moraq) | scoured | Intensive scouring of bronze vessel; hapax; LXX renders more generally as "poured out" |
| תָּמִיד (tamid) | continually / regular | Perpetual, ongoing obligation; also used for the "regular" daily offerings; LXX: διαπαντός |
| בַּקֹּדֶשׁ (ba-qodesh) | in the Holy Place | The inner sanctuary where atonement blood is applied; LXX: ἐν τῷ ἁγίῳ |
| עֹלָה (olah) | burnt offering | Established from Lev 1 |
| מִנְחָה (minchah) | grain offering | Established from Lev 2 |
| חַטָּאת (chattat) | sin offering | Established from Lev 4; LXX: περὶ τῆς ἁμαρτίας |
| אָשָׁם (asham) | guilt offering | Established from Lev 5 |
| שְׁלָמִים (shelamim) | peace offerings | Established from Lev 3; LXX: σωτήριον |
| אִשֶּׁה (isheh) | fire offering | Established from Lev 1; LXX: κάρπωμα |
| רֵיחַ נִיחֹחַ (re'ach nichoach) | pleasing aroma | Anthropomorphic idiom of divine acceptance; LXX: ὀσμὴν εὐωδίας |
| אַזְכָּרָה (azkarah) | memorial portion | The handful burned on the altar; LXX: μνημόσυνον |
| כִּפֶּר (kipper) | make atonement | Central Levitical atonement verb; LXX: ἐξιλάσεται |
| יהוה | the LORD | YHWH — consistent throughout |
| אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד | tent of meeting | Established convention |

— LOGOS
